hard.next_step: Zycus to maintain light-touch engagement with Benjamin through LinkedIn, email, and future webinar/product update invitations. Re-engage closer to SAP Ariba renewal or when QuantumScape begins evaluating intake providers more seriously. Share relevant Merlin Intake content focused on Microsoft Teams intake, SAP/Ariba integration, quote upload, punchouts, and non-catalog purchasing workflows.
hard.ais_why: QuantumScape's Procurement Operations Manager is dissatisfied with Ariba, seeking AI-assisted solutions; Merlin and Merlin Intake are scoped and the compelling event is an upcoming SAP Ariba renewal; engagement is exploratory with no budget discussed.
hard.competitor: SAP Ariba (incumbent); Status quo / no-decision; Zip (prior evaluation)
hard.primary_competitor: Status quo (do-nothing) / SAP Ariba incumbent inertia

north_star_verdict.headline: Exploratory single-threaded nurture: after a positive 26 Jun Merlin Intake demo, Benjamin Sheres explicitly deferred any project until an undated SAP Ariba renewal, with no budget, no economic buyer engaged, and no evaluation timeline.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 25 Jun 2026, moved Initial Interest to Qualified on 30 Jun. Close date 30 Jun 2027 is a ~12-month placeholder, not a committed date. Last genuine buyer touch 26 Jun discovery+demo call (10 days ago as of 6 Jul); the 30 Jun 'Call Attempted / Opportunity Health' tasks are system-generated, not buyer engagement. Time-in-stage ~6 days at Qualified, no slip history. Pace: buyer explicitly stated no near-term project and no bandwidth; deal is on hold awaiting an undated Ariba renewal forcing event. Pipeline forecast is defensible for an exploratory, on-hold deal with no committed timeline.
evidence: Benjamin Sheres on the 26 Jun demo: 'I don't think that is in the books right now... our renewal is coming up pretty quickly, and I don't think that we're gonna have the bandwidth to do a project right now' and 'at some point, there may be a breaking point where... let's really run a serious RFP... but I don't think that we're there right now'.

Benjamin validated Merlin Intake fit positively ('I like that it lives within Teams, which we are a Teams organization', 'the way that this looks is relatively intuitive', 'very similar to Zip') but requested no next meeting and asked only to 'keep in touch' (26 Jun).
No economic buyer, decision maker, or multi-threading beyond Benjamin (Procurement Operations Manager); zero OpportunityContactRoles on the opp; Kevin Hettrich cold-call could-not-connect on 26 Jun.
SAP Ariba renewal date unknown: Benjamin said the renewal is 'coming up pretty quickly' but gave no date, so re-engagement cannot be timed (26 Jun).

Benjamin stated no budget and no bandwidth for a project now, and prior Zip evaluation was blocked by 'internal organizational factors' plus significant sunk investment in getting Ariba working (26 Jun).
Recent IT turnover noted: 'we've had some turnover in our IT organization, so we're looking around again' (26 Jun), which reopens the window but reduces implementation bandwidth.

metrics.narrative: QuantumScape runs approximately 9,000 purchase orders a year, split roughly 50/50 between SG&A overhead and a long tail of materials, equipment and R&D spend, in a decentralized model where engineers and scientists run their own requisitions, onboarding and supplier follow-up. Benjamin Sheres named the core financial pain on 26 Jun: 'SAP Ariba charges you based on how much spend you're ordering in that platform, we wanna move away from that and go into SAP,' i.e. escape Ariba's spend-based fees by moving PR creation into SAP while keeping an intake layer. The pain is real and specific but not yet quantified: no dollar figure for the Ariba fees, the audit labour overhead, or a target saving was stated. Quantifying the Ariba fee and audit-labour savings is the move that would convert this qualitative pain into an internal business case.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is engaged. Benjamin Sheres is a Procurement Operations Manager with no budget authority; commercials, pricing and budget were never discussed on the 26 Jun call and Benjamin was explicit there is 'no bandwidth to do a project right now.' The budget decision is effectively the SAP Ariba renewal, whose owner sits in finance/executive leadership and has not been reached: Kevin Hettrich (a real activity contact) was targeted by a 26 Jun Nooks cold call that logged 'Could Not Connect.' The evidence points to the Ariba renewal budget owner as the true economic buyer, but they are unmapped and unengaged; reaching that finance owner with a quantified Ariba-fee-savings case is the ranked move.
sources: SF Task 26 Jun 2026 (Nooks call, Kevin Hettrich, Could Not Connect)

decision_criteria.narrative: Benjamin articulated clear functional criteria while pressure-testing the demo on 26 Jun: intake-only (not a full closed P2P suite), Microsoft Teams-native intake ('I like that it lives within Teams, which we are a Teams organization'), quote/OCR upload for non-catalog and mid-tail spend, punch-out capability ('most common punch out people actually use'), SAP/ERP integration to move PR creation into SAP, configurable approval and change-order workflows, and supplier recommendations from preferred/negotiated suppliers. He also set an implicit benchmark against Zip, which he had previously judged 'a great solution.' What is not yet defined is a formal weighted evaluation or RFP scorecard, because the buyer is pre-project; these criteria are confirmed as needs but not yet as a scored selection process.

decision_process.narrative: There is no active decision process. The 26 Jun call was a single discovery+demo with Benjamin, Steve Ovadje (owner) and a Zycus SE; no other buyer stakeholders joined and no next meeting was requested. Benjamin described the likely future path as reactive to the Ariba renewal: 'at some point, there may be a breaking point where... let's really run a serious RFP... but I don't think that we're there right now.' So the process today is 'do nothing until the renewal forces an intake evaluation,' with an unknown renewal date. Getting the renewal date in writing and understanding who runs any future RFP is what would create process visibility.

paper_process.narrative: No paper process exists yet: no MSA, order form, SOW, InfoSec review, legal or procurement mechanics have started, consistent with an exploratory Qualified-stage deal. Implementation was discussed only in the abstract, with the SE estimating '3 months to 6 months' depending on complexity and integration. The only forcing function is the SAP Ariba renewal, whose date is unknown, so there is no contract clock to work against. Visibility would come from pinning the Ariba renewal/contract-expiration date and scoping the SAP integration effort once a project is real.
sources: Avoma demo call 26 Jun 2026 (Zycus SE and Benjamin Sheres)

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is confirmed and owned by Benjamin Sheres, who has driven procurement automation at QuantumScape for four years. Two pains are explicit: (1) Ariba's spend-based charging model, which he wants to escape by moving PR creation into SAP; and (2) control gaps from a decentralized, engineer-led buying model that forces the small procurement team into heavy auditing: 'we as a procurement organization have been needing to audit them and make sure that they are making the requisition right... we need some tools on our side to set them up for success.' He wants to funnel non-catalog spend to procurement efficiently while leaving punch-out/catalog buying decentralized. The pain is genuine but currently outranked by inertia: the existing platform 'is functional' and 'nobody's rushing' given the sunk Ariba investment.

champion.narrative: Benjamin Sheres is a developing but weak champion. He is the engaged pain owner, drove the discovery conversation, and reacted positively to Merlin Intake (Teams-native, intuitive, comparable to Zip). What has developed: genuine product interest and a candid share of QuantumScape's internal context and constraints. What is missing: he has no budget authority, gave no access to an economic buyer or IT leadership, made no internal introductions, and explicitly capped expectations ('I don't wanna have you be thinking that we're gonna sign a contract or run a whole project team anytime soon'). His engagement is passive and exploratory and at risk of cooling while the deal waits on the undated Ariba renewal.

competition.narrative: The strongest current threat is not a named rival but the status quo: QuantumScape keeps SAP Ariba by default because it 'is functional,' they have 'made significant investment in getting Ariba working,' and 'nobody's rushing.' Ariba is the incumbent being displaced on its spend-based fee pain, but sunk cost plus recent IT turnover previously killed a switch to Zip despite Benjamin judging Zip 'a great solution.' Zip is therefore a dormant prior alternative likely to reappear if a serious RFP ever launches. Our wedge is intake-only optionality (far lower switching cost than a full Ariba rip-out) plus SAP/ERP integration via iSaaS to move PR creation into SAP and eliminate Ariba spend-based fees, positioned as a layer that works with SAP rather than a rip-and-replace.

deal_movement.summary: One genuine buyer interaction cycle: an email introduction on 25 Jun led into a 26 Jun discovery+demo where the buyer validated fit but deferred; the 30 Jun changes were an internal stage/owner cleanup, and the 30 Jun 'Opportunity Health' tasks are system-generated, not buyer engagement.

items.change: Davis Thomson introduced QuantumScape (Benjamin Sheres) via the Procurement Round Table thread; Steve Ovadje and Prashant Saxena engaged and booked the demo; QS context shared (400 users placing requests, 1 buyer, ~50% punch-out, cXML invoicing).
Discovery + Merlin Intake demo held; Benjamin validated Teams-native intake, punch-outs, OCR quote upload and approval workflows, but closed by deferring any project until the Ariba renewal.
Stage moved Initial Interest to Qualified and ownership reassigned to Steve Ovadje (internal record update, not buyer-driven).

competitive_position.summary: The single strongest CURRENT threat is do-nothing/status-quo: QuantumScape's Ariba platform 'is functional,' they carry heavy sunk investment, and Benjamin explicitly deferred any project (26 Jun) with no forcing date. SAP Ariba is the incumbent being displaced on spend-based-fee pain but is the default winner if inertia holds. Zip was liked in a prior evaluation ('I thought it was a great solution') but was blocked by internal factors and is dormant, likely to resurface in any future serious RFP. No active competitive bake-off is running today.

competitors.name: Status quo / Do nothing (Ariba auto-renewal default)
competitors.quote: Our renewal is coming up pretty quickly, and I don't think that we're gonna have the bandwidth to do a project right now. At some point, there may be a breaking point where let's really run a serious RFP, but I don't think that we're there right now.
competitors.how_we_win: Inertia, not a rival, is what loses this deal: pin the Ariba renewal date, quantify the spend-based-fee and audit-labour savings, and get that case in front of the finance/renewal budget owner so the renewal is not rubber-stamped.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: SAP Ariba charges you based on how much spend you're ordering in that platform, we wanna move away from that and go into SAP. We've already made significant investment in getting Ariba working, and internal organizational factors made it difficult to switch platforms.
competitors.how_we_win: Position Merlin Intake as an intake-only layer that works with SAP (via iSaaS/API integration) to move PR creation into SAP and eliminate Ariba spend-based fees, avoiding a full rip-and-replace and its switching cost; quantify the fee savings to the budget owner.

competitors.name: Zip
competitors.quote: Honestly, in my opinion, I thought it was a great solution. But internal organizational factors made it difficult to switch platforms because we've already made significant investment in getting Ariba working.
competitors.how_we_win: Zip was viewed positively but lost to Ariba inertia; anchor Merlin's comparable AI/Teams-native intake and intuitive UI as on par with Zip, then differentiate on full-suite optionality and SAP integration depth versus Zip's intake-only pure-play.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong functional fit on intake usability and Teams-native experience; the exposure is on switching effort and timing, not capability. Benjamin pressure-tested the product hard and came away positive, but the organization's bandwidth and sunk Ariba investment keep it exploratory.

items.criterion: Intake-only, not a full closed P2P suite
items.quote: We are a full system solution. However, we can provide just the intake.
I like that it lives within Teams, which we are a Teams organization.
No. It does have punch out capabilities... we utilize that pretty thoroughly.

items.criterion: SAP/ERP integration to move PR creation into SAP
items.quote: We have a middleware platform called iSaaS, and we have API tools to do the integration if you're just doing a transition.

items.criterion: Low switching cost and low implementation bandwidth
items.quote: I could answer you for 3 months to 6 months, and it really depends on what's being done... I don't think that we're gonna have the bandwidth to do a project right now.

items.deliverable: Send Benjamin targeted Merlin Intake content (Teams intake, SAP/Ariba integration, quote upload, punchouts, non-catalog) and webinar invites.
items.grounding_quote: I'll try to have Prashant invite you to webinars we might be having just in case you wanna join and see what else we have cooking in store.
items.source: Steve Ovadje, call 26 Jun 2026; SF Next_Step__c

items.quote: Our renewal is coming up pretty quickly... but I don't think that we're there right now.
[Nooks Call] Could Not Connect - Kevin Hettrich
I don't think that is in the books right now.

items.area: Quantified business case (Ariba fee / audit-labour savings)
items.quote: SAP Ariba charges you based on how much spend you're ordering in that platform.

best_practice_check.summary: This is an exploratory, single-threaded nurture whose fate turns on one forcing event and one power gap; the win levers are timing visibility, multi-threading to finance, and a quantified case.
flags: Pin the SAP Ariba renewal/contract-expiration date in writing from Benjamin: it is the only forcing event and re-engagement is blind without it (26 Jun).
Multi-thread to the finance/renewal budget owner: today we are single-threaded through Benjamin, a manager with no budget authority, and the Kevin Hettrich cold-call could not connect (26 Jun).
Build a quantified Ariba spend-based-fee and audit-labour savings case to convert Benjamin's product interest into internal urgency ahead of the renewal.

items.name: Benjamin Sheres
items.title: Procurement Operations Manager

items.risk: Developing but weak and at risk of cooling: no budget authority, no EB/IT access, no internal advocacy, deal on hold awaiting the undated Ariba renewal.

items.name: Kevin Hettrich
items.risk: Likely finance/renewal budget owner but unmapped and unengaged; a 26 Jun Nooks cold call logged 'Could Not Connect.' Title unconfirmed in Salesforce; do not assert a title until verified.

champion_strength.summary: Benjamin Sheres (Procurement Operations Manager) is a developing but weak champion: engaged pain owner who validated Merlin Intake fit and shared internal context, but has no budget authority, no access to an economic buyer or IT leadership, made no introductions, and explicitly capped the deal ('I don't wanna have you thinking that we're gonna sign a contract... anytime soon'). His engagement is passive and exploratory and at risk of cooling while the deal waits on the undated Ariba renewal.
champion_strength.source: Avoma discovery call 26 Jun 2026; SF AIS_Why__c

ai_positioning_strength.summary: The Zycus SE walked Benjamin through both the generative-AI (request understanding, OCR quote parsing) and agentic (next-step guidance, catalog/punch-out steering, supplier recommendations) layers of Merlin Intake, and Benjamin engaged with them, but his enthusiasm attached to practical outcomes (Teams-native intake, intuitive UI) rather than the AI story itself, and no quantified AI value was landed. The agentic narrative is present but not yet a differentiated, quantified wedge.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 6.0 / Moderate is accurate: Benjamin is 'dissatisfied with Ariba, seeking AI-assisted solutions' and probed the generative/agentic intake capabilities with interest, but the engagement is exploratory with no budget and no agentic-platform commitment, keeping demonstrated appetite in the curious band rather than hungry.

The sole forcing event (SAP Ariba renewal) has no known date, so re-engagement cannot be timed; primary threat is no-decision/status-quo.
Heavy sunk investment in Ariba plus recent IT turnover previously blocked a switch to Zip ('internal organizational factors') and reduces implementation bandwidth.
Single-threaded through one procurement manager with no OpportunityContactRoles on the opp; finance and IT decision-makers are unmapped and unengaged.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is cooling by the buyer's own words: one substantive call, no next meeting requested, and an explicit ask to defer to the Ariba renewal. The deal is alive as a nurture but not progressing.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: A single exploratory Merlin Intake demo landed well on usability, but Benjamin Sheres explicitly deferred any project until an undated SAP Ariba renewal; the deal is a single-threaded nurture with no budget, no economic buyer engaged, and no timeline.

win_position.text: Benjamin Sheres liked Merlin Intake (Teams-native, intuitive, on par with Zip) on the 26 Jun demo but closed by deferring: 'I don't think that is in the books right now... we're not gonna have the bandwidth to do a project right now,' so this sits as an early nurture, not an active deal.

No budget and no economic buyer engaged: Benjamin has no spend authority and the likely renewal budget owner (Kevin Hettrich) could not be reached on a 26 Jun cold call, leaving us single-threaded through a manager.
Inertia is the main thing that can kill this: heavy sunk cost in Ariba plus recent IT turnover already blocked a switch to Zip ('internal organizational factors', 26 Jun).
There is a real re-entry pain: ~9,000 POs/year, decentralized engineer-led buying, and Ariba's spend-based fees that Benjamin wants to escape by moving PR creation into SAP (26 Jun).

deal_momentum.text: Momentum is flat: the buyer declined a next meeting and asked only to 'keep in touch' and re-engage near the Ariba renewal (26 Jun).
No commercial motion: no pricing, security, or legal discussion, and no customer-named date; the renewal is only 'coming up pretty quickly' with no date given (26 Jun).
One substantive discovery+demo did happen on 26 Jun with deep product probing (punch-outs, approval workflows, change orders, OCR trust), showing genuine curiosity.

Single-threaded with no contact roles on the opp: only Benjamin (Procurement Operations Manager) is engaged, with no EB or IT sign-off mapped.
Bandwidth is the blocker: recent IT turnover and existing Ariba investment mean no internal capacity for a project now (26 Jun).
